title: What is Python's descriptor protocol?
description: How do descriptors work in Python?
options: Descriptors define __get__, __set__, and/or __delete__ methods to customize attribute access; property(), classmethod(), staticmethod() are built-in descriptors. Data descriptors (with __set__) take precedence over instance __dict__
correct_answer: Descriptors define __get__, __set__, and/or __delete__ methods to customize attribute access; property(), classmethod(), staticmethod() are built-in descriptors. Data descriptors (with __set__) take precedence over instance __dict__

title: What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__?
description: When is __str__ vs __repr__ called?
options: __repr__ returns an unambiguous developer-friendly string (used by repr(), debugger); __str__ returns a readable user-friendly string (used by str(), print()). If __str__ is missing, Python falls back to __repr__
correct_answer: __repr__ returns an unambiguous developer-friendly string (used by repr(), debugger); __str__ returns a readable user-friendly string (used by str(), print()). If __str__ is missing, Python falls back to __repr__

title: What is Python's MRO (Method Resolution Order)?
description: How does Python resolve method calls in multiple inheritance?
options: Python uses C3 linearization algorithm to determine method resolution order; checks classes left-to-right, depth-first, but respects monotonicity. View with ClassName.__mro__ or ClassName.mro()
correct_answer: Python uses C3 linearization algorithm to determine method resolution order; checks classes left-to-right, depth-first, but respects monotonicity. View with ClassName.__mro__ or ClassName.mro()

title: What are Python slots?
description: What does __slots__ do?
options: __slots__ = ('x', 'y') restricts instance attributes to listed names, removes __dict__ overhead; reduces memory usage significantly for many instances. Cannot add arbitrary attributes
correct_answer: __slots__ = ('x', 'y') restricts instance attributes to listed names, removes __dict__ overhead; reduces memory usage significantly for many instances. Cannot add arbitrary attributes

title: What is the difference between is and == in Python?
description: How do 'is' and '==' differ?
options: 'is' checks identity (same object in memory, using id()); '==' checks equality (same value, using __eq__). Use 'is' for None, True, False comparisons; '==' for value comparison
correct_answer: 'is' checks identity (same object in memory, using id()); '==' checks equality (same value, using __eq__). Use 'is' for None, True, False comparisons; '==' for value comparison

title: What are Python's magic methods for comparison?
description: List the comparison dunder methods.
options: __eq__ (==), __ne__ (!=), __lt__ (<), __le__ (<=), __gt__ (>), __ge__ (>=). Use @functools.total_ordering decorator to generate all from __eq__ and one other
correct_answer: __eq__ (==), __ne__ (!=), __lt__ (<), __le__ (<=), __gt__ (>), __ge__ (>=). Use @functools.total_ordering decorator to generate all from __eq__ and one other

title: What is the GIL and how to work around it?
description: How do you achieve parallelism despite the GIL?
options: GIL allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. Workarounds: multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks, asyncio for I/O-bound, C extensions that release GIL, concurrent.futures for both patterns
correct_answer: GIL allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. Workarounds: multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks, asyncio for I/O-bound, C extensions that release GIL, concurrent.futures for both patterns

title: Implement an async task queue in Python.
description: Create an async producer-consumer task queue.
correct_answer:
import asyncio

class AsyncTaskQueue:
    def __init__(self, max_workers=3):
        self.queue = asyncio.Queue()
        self.max_workers = max_workers

    async def worker(self):
        while True:
            task = await self.queue.get()
            try:
                await task
            finally:
                self.queue.task_done()

    async def add_task(self, coro):
        await self.queue.put(coro)

    async def run(self):
        workers = [asyncio.create_task(self.worker()) for _ in range(self.max_workers)]
        await self.queue.join()
        for w in workers: w.cancel()

title: What is the difference between list and deque?
description: When should you use deque instead of list?
options: deque (double-ended queue) has O(1) append/pop from both ends; list has O(n) insert/pop at index 0. Use deque for queues, BFS, sliding windows. list is better for random access O(1)
correct_answer: deque (double-ended queue) has O(1) append/pop from both ends; list has O(n) insert/pop at index 0. Use deque for queues, BFS, sliding windows. list is better for random access O(1)

title: What are Python comprehensions vs generator expressions?
description: Compare list comps and genexps.
options: List comprehension [x for x in ...] creates a full list in memory; generator expression (x for x in ...) yields values lazily one at a time. Genexps save memory for large datasets; use list comp when you need the full list
correct_answer: List comprehension [x for x in ...] creates a full list in memory; generator expression (x for x in ...) yields values lazily one at a time. Genexps save memory for large datasets; use list comp when you need the full list

title: What is Python's asyncio event loop?
description: How does the asyncio event loop work?
options: The event loop runs coroutines, handles I/O callbacks, and schedules tasks. asyncio.run() creates and manages the loop; await yields control back to the loop; loop multiplexes I/O using selectors (epoll/kqueue)
correct_answer: The event loop runs coroutines, handles I/O callbacks, and schedules tasks. asyncio.run() creates and manages the loop; await yields control back to the loop; loop multiplexes I/O using selectors (epoll/kqueue)

title: Implement a thread-safe bounded buffer.
description: Create a producer-consumer buffer with threading.
correct_answer:
import threading

class BoundedBuffer:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.buffer = []
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.lock = threading.Lock()
        self.not_empty = threading.Condition(self.lock)
        self.not_full = threading.Condition(self.lock)

    def put(self, item):
        with self.not_full:
            while len(self.buffer) >= self.capacity:
                self.not_full.wait()
            self.buffer.append(item)
            self.not_empty.notify()

    def get(self):
        with self.not_empty:
            while not self.buffer:
                self.not_empty.wait()
            item = self.buffer.pop(0)
            self.not_full.notify()
            return item

title: What is the difference between @property and __getattr__?
description: Compare property and attribute access customization.
options: @property defines managed attributes on specific names with getter/setter/deleter; __getattr__ is called only when normal attribute lookup fails (fallback). __getattribute__ intercepts ALL attribute access (rarely used)
correct_answer: @property defines managed attributes on specific names with getter/setter/deleter; __getattr__ is called only when normal attribute lookup fails (fallback). __getattribute__ intercepts ALL attribute access (rarely used)

title: Implement a Python dataclass with custom ordering.
description: Create a dataclass with comparison support.
correct_answer:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass(order=True)
class Student:
    sort_index: float = field(init=False, repr=False)
    name: str
    grade: float
    age: int

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.sort_index = -self.grade  # sort by grade descending

# students = [Student('Alice', 3.9, 20), Student('Bob', 3.7, 21)]
# sorted(students)  # Alice first (higher grade)

title: What is monkey patching in Python?
description: What is monkey patching and when is it used?
options: Dynamically modifying a class or module at runtime by replacing attributes/methods; commonly used in testing (mocking), fixing third-party bugs, or adding functionality. Can make code harder to debug and maintain
correct_answer: Dynamically modifying a class or module at runtime by replacing attributes/methods; commonly used in testing (mocking), fixing third-party bugs, or adding functionality. Can make code harder to debug and maintain

title: Implement a Python context manager for timing.
description: Create a context manager that measures execution time.
correct_answer:
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def timer(label='Block'):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        print(f'{label} took {elapsed:.4f}s')

# Usage: with timer('Data processing'): heavy_computation()

title: What are Python's structural pattern matching (match/case)?
description: How does match/case work in Python 3.10+?
options: match/case enables structural pattern matching; supports literal, capture, wildcard (_), OR (|), guard (if), sequence, mapping, and class patterns. More powerful than switch — can destructure and bind variables
correct_answer: match/case enables structural pattern matching; supports literal, capture, wildcard (_), OR (|), guard (if), sequence, mapping, and class patterns. More powerful than switch — can destructure and bind variables
